figures.caption: Figure 5: Qualitative 5122 samples: RaPD vs. pixel-diffusion baselines [52, 51, 68].
Table 1: Text-to-image generation on GenEval [22] (512× 512). ‡ indicates our re-runs of released models under the same seed and environment.

Figure 11: Additional text-to-image generation results of RaPD. All samples share the same model and a single denoised NIF latent per prompt; different output sizes are obtained by varying only the CQAR query coordinate grid. Across diverse content categories and aspect ratios, RaPD produces visually faithful and structurally coherent images.
Figure 2: Reconstruction–generation gap of existing NIF representations: strong super-resolution reconstruction (top) but weak text-to-image generation (bottom).
Table 5: RaPD model specifications and training hyperparameters.
Figure 6: Arbitrary-resolution generation. (A) GenEval [22] prompts rendered at multiple output scales. (B) DPG-Bench [31] examples showing strong long-form prompt fidelity.
Table 2: Text-to-image generation results on DPG-Bench [31] (512×512). ‡ denotes results obtained by running the released models with the same random seed and environment as RaPD.

Figure 3: Overview of RaPD. (A) Stage-1: semantically guided multi-resolution NIF learning. (B) Stage-2: text-conditioned flow matching in normalized NIF latents. (C) Inference: render one denoised latent at arbitrary resolutions via CQAR.
Figure 8: Generation under different timestep shift (prompt: “a photo of a surfboard / handbag ”).
Figure 7: Ablations of Semantic Distillation and CQAR.

Figure 10: Additional qualitative text-to-image comparisons between RaPD and baselines. Each row corresponds to a different prompt; each column shows the output of one method at the same target resolution. RaPD better preserves fine-grained detail and prompt fidelity, especially at higher resolutions.
Figure 4: Implicit decoder comparison. (A) LIIF: per-pixel MLP. (B) CLIF: shallow CNN. (C) CQAR: coordinate-conditioned windowed attention over output tokens.
